title: Blood glucose control — diabetes and insulin
examTip: Blood glucose control is a guaranteed B5 topic — AQA has asked about it in almost every past paper. Know the negative feedback loop for both high and low glucose, and be completely fluent on the difference between Type 1 and Type 2 diabetes. These are the marks everyone should be getting.
introTips: You're going to learn how your body keeps blood glucose at just the right level all day, every day — and what happens when that system breaks down.
Negative feedback is the key idea here. Every control mechanism in this topic follows the same pattern: too high → bring it down; too low → bring it up.
Type 1 and Type 2 diabetes come up separately and together in exam questions. Make sure you can explain the cause and treatment of each — they are not the same condition.
intro: Every meal you eat sends a wave of glucose into your blood. Every burst of exercise pulls glucose out. Yet your blood glucose level stays remarkably stable — because your body is constantly monitoring it and correcting it using hormones from the pancreas. When this system fails, diabetes results. Understanding how the normal system works, and precisely how each type of diabetes differs, is one of the most reliably tested areas in the entire GCSE Biology specification.

keyPoints.heading: Why blood glucose must be controlled
keyPoints.content: Glucose is the main fuel for cellular respiration in every cell in your body. But blood glucose concentration must stay within a narrow range. If it's too high, water moves out of cells by osmosis (because the blood has a lower water potential than normal), causing cells to shrink and become dehydrated. Glucose is also excreted in urine, causing excessive thirst and frequent urination. If it's too low, cells don't have enough glucose for respiration — brain cells are especially vulnerable, leading to confusion, loss of consciousness, and in severe cases, coma. The pancreas is the organ that detects blood glucose levels and responds by secreting hormones to correct any deviation.
writeBullets: Blood glucose must stay within a narrow range for cells to function normally
Too high: cells dehydrate (osmosis), glucose excreted in urine, excessive thirst
Too low: insufficient glucose for respiration — brain cells most vulnerable
The pancreas monitors blood glucose and secretes corrective hormones

keyTerms.def: The amount of glucose dissolved in the blood — must be maintained within a narrow range for normal cell function
The organ that monitors blood glucose levels and secretes insulin and glucagon to regulate them

keyPoints.memTip: The pancreas is the blood glucose monitor. Think of it as a thermostat for sugar — it detects the level and sends out insulin or glucagon to adjust it. Fix this image: pancreas = glucose controller.

keyPoints.heading: Insulin — lowering blood glucose
keyPoints.content: When blood glucose rises above the set point (e.g. after a meal), the beta cells in the pancreas detect this and secrete insulin into the blood. Insulin travels to the liver and muscles, causing them to take up glucose from the blood and convert it to glycogen for storage. This process is called glycogenesis. Cells throughout the body also increase their uptake of glucose for respiration. As blood glucose falls back to the set point, the pancreas detects this and reduces insulin secretion. This is a classic example of negative feedback — the response reduces the change that triggered it.
writeBullets: Blood glucose rises → beta cells in pancreas detect this → insulin secreted
Insulin causes liver and muscles to convert glucose → glycogen (glycogenesis)
Cells increase glucose uptake for respiration
Blood glucose falls back to set point → insulin secretion reduced
This is negative feedback — the response counteracts the original change

keyTerms.def: Hormone secreted by the pancreas when blood glucose is high; causes glucose to be converted to glycogen and stored
The storage form of glucose in the liver and muscles
The conversion of glucose to glycogen — happens in the liver and muscles when blood glucose is high
A control mechanism where a change triggers a response that reverses the change, maintaining a stable set point
Cells in the pancreas that detect high blood glucose and secrete insulin

keyPoints.memTip: Insulin = storage. High glucose → insulin → store it as glycogen. 'Insulin moves glucose IN to storage.' The 'IN' in insulin = INto storage. Glyco-GENESIS = creating glycogen. When blood glucose is high, the body is building (genesis) glycogen.

keyPoints.heading: Glucagon — raising blood glucose
keyPoints.content: When blood glucose falls below the set point (e.g. during exercise or fasting), the alpha cells in the pancreas detect this and secrete glucagon into the blood. Glucagon travels to the liver, which responds by breaking down stored glycogen back into glucose and releasing it into the blood. This process is called glycogenolysis. As blood glucose rises back to the set point, glucagon secretion decreases. Again, this is negative feedback. Insulin and glucagon work as an antagonistic pair — they have opposite effects, one lowering blood glucose and the other raising it, allowing precise control around the set point.
writeBullets: Blood glucose falls → alpha cells in pancreas detect this → glucagon secreted
Glucagon causes liver to convert glycogen → glucose (glycogenolysis)
Glucose released into blood → blood glucose rises back to set point
Glucagon secretion decreases as blood glucose returns to normal
Insulin and glucagon are an antagonistic pair — opposite effects, precise control

keyTerms.def: Hormone secreted by the pancreas when blood glucose is low; causes glycogen to be broken down to glucose
The breakdown of glycogen to glucose — happens in the liver when blood glucose is low
Two hormones with opposite effects working together to maintain a stable level — e.g. insulin and glucagon
Cells in the pancreas that detect low blood glucose and secrete glucagon

keyPoints.memTip: Glucagon = release. Low glucose → glucagon → release glucose from glycogen. 'GlucaGON releases glucose — it's GONE from storage.' Glyco-LYSIS = breaking down (lysis = breaking). When blood glucose is low, the body is breaking (lysis) glycogen to release glucose.

keyPoints.heading: Type 1 and Type 2 diabetes — causes and treatments
keyPoints.content: Type 1 diabetes is an autoimmune condition where the immune system destroys the beta cells in the pancreas. As a result, the pancreas produces little or no insulin. Blood glucose can rise to dangerous levels after meals and cannot be brought down. It is treated with insulin injections (the patient injects insulin before meals to replace what the body cannot produce). Type 2 diabetes develops when the body's cells become resistant to insulin — they stop responding properly to it, so blood glucose regulation breaks down. The pancreas may still produce insulin but the cells don't respond. It is strongly associated with obesity and an unhealthy diet. It is managed through diet and exercise (reducing carbohydrate intake, losing weight) and sometimes medication to improve insulin sensitivity.
writeBullets: Type 1: immune system destroys beta cells → no insulin produced
Type 1 treatment: insulin injections (replaces the missing insulin)
Type 2: cells become resistant to insulin → blood glucose not regulated
Type 2 risk factors: obesity, poor diet, lack of exercise
Type 2 management: diet, exercise, medication (not usually insulin injections)
Both types result in inability to control blood glucose, but for different reasons

keyTerms.def: An autoimmune condition where beta cells are destroyed, so no insulin is produced; treated with insulin injections
A condition where body cells become resistant to insulin; linked to obesity and unhealthy diet; managed with diet, exercise, and medication
The treatment for Type 1 diabetes — replacing the insulin the pancreas cannot produce
A condition where body cells fail to respond normally to insulin — the cause of Type 2 diabetes

keyPoints.memTip: Type 1 = No insulin (pancreas broken). Type 2 = Ignores insulin (cells broken). Type 1 needs injections because the pancreas doesn't make any. Type 2 is managed by lifestyle because the pancreas still works — cells just don't listen. Keep the distinction: pancreas broken (1) vs cells broken (2).

tables.title: Type 1 vs Type 2 diabetes
rows: Immune system destroys beta cells — no insulin produced
Body cells become resistant to insulin
Often normal or elevated, but ineffective
Obesity, unhealthy diet, lack of exercise
Diet, exercise, medication — not usually insulin
More common in adults (but increasing in younger people)

0.question: What happens when blood glucose levels rise above the set point?
options: The pancreas secretes glucagon, which converts glucose to glycogen
The pancreas secretes insulin, which causes the liver to convert glucose to glycogen
The adrenal glands release adrenaline to lower glucose levels
The liver releases glucose into the blood to raise the level further
0.correctFeedback: ✅ Correct — high blood glucose → insulin secreted by pancreas → liver (and muscles) convert glucose to glycogen (glycogenesis). Blood glucose falls back to set point.
0.wrongFeedback: High blood glucose triggers insulin release from the pancreas (not glucagon — that's for low glucose). Insulin causes the liver to convert glucose to glycogen (glycogenesis), storing the excess. The adrenal glands and adrenaline are not involved in blood glucose regulation in this way.

2.question: Why does blood glucose fall when glucagon is released?
options: Glucagon causes the liver to convert glucose to glycogen
Glucagon causes the liver to convert glycogen back to glucose and release it into the blood — this raises blood glucose from a low level back to normal
Glucagon is released when blood glucose is high, and it lowers it by promoting respiration
Glucagon is released when blood glucose is high and stores it as glycogen
2.correctFeedback: ✅ Right, though note the question was slightly tricky — glucagon RAISES blood glucose (by breaking down glycogen), not lowers it. You correctly identified what glucagon does. It's released when glucose is LOW to bring it back UP.
2.wrongFeedback: This is a common confusion. Glucagon is released when blood glucose is LOW. It causes the liver to break down glycogen into glucose (glycogenolysis) and release it into the blood — this RAISES blood glucose back to the set point. Glucagon raises glucose. Insulin lowers glucose.

3.question: A patient is diagnosed with Type 1 diabetes. What is the most likely cause and the appropriate treatment?
options: Cells have become resistant to insulin; treated with diet and exercise
The immune system has destroyed beta cells in the pancreas; treated with insulin injections
The pancreas produces too much insulin; treated by reducing carbohydrate intake
The liver cannot store glycogen; treated with glucagon injections
3.correctFeedback: ✅ Exactly right — Type 1 is an autoimmune destruction of beta cells, meaning no insulin is produced. The treatment is daily insulin injections to replace what the body cannot make.
3.wrongFeedback: Type 1 diabetes occurs when the immune system destroys the beta cells in the pancreas, so no insulin is produced. The treatment is insulin injections — not diet or exercise, which are management strategies for Type 2. Type 2 involves insulin resistance, not absent insulin production.

commonMistakes: Confusing insulin and glucagon — insulin lowers blood glucose (converts glucose to glycogen); glucagon raises blood glucose (breaks glycogen back to glucose). Reversing these two completely ruins the answer.
Saying glucagon is released when blood glucose is high — it's the opposite. Glucagon is released when blood glucose is LOW. Insulin is released when it's HIGH.

Mixing up Type 1 and Type 2 — Type 1 = no insulin produced (autoimmune); Type 2 = insulin resistance (lifestyle-linked). The treatments are different: Type 1 must have injections; Type 2 is usually managed with diet/exercise.
Forgetting to name the specific process — say 'glycogenesis' (glucose → glycogen) and 'glycogenolysis' (glycogen → glucose). Using these terms shows precise understanding and can earn bonus marks.

Not identifying the pancreas as both the sensor and the responder — it detects blood glucose AND secretes the correcting hormone. Some students think the liver detects it. The pancreas does both jobs.
examTips: For the negative feedback loop, describe it in a clear sequence: (1) blood glucose rises → (2) pancreas detects this → (3) insulin secreted → (4) liver converts glucose to glycogen → (5) blood glucose returns to set point → (6) insulin secretion decreases. All six steps, all marks.
When comparing Type 1 and Type 2, give cause AND treatment for each — not just one or the other. Four pieces of information = four potential marks.

If asked why insulin injections are used for Type 1 but not usually for Type 2, the answer is that Type 1 patients cannot produce insulin (pancreas destroyed), so it must be supplied externally. Type 2 patients still produce insulin — the problem is cellular resistance.
AQA sometimes asks 'explain what is meant by negative feedback' — say: a change from the set point triggers a response that reverses the change, returning the variable to the set point.
revisionCardBullets: Blood glucose controlled by negative feedback involving the pancreas
High glucose → pancreas releases insulin → liver converts glucose to glycogen (glycogenesis)
Low glucose → pancreas releases glucagon → liver breaks glycogen to glucose (glycogenolysis)
Insulin and glucagon are an antagonistic pair — opposite effects, precise control
Beta cells in pancreas secrete insulin; alpha cells secrete glucagon
Type 1 diabetes: immune system destroys beta cells → no insulin → must inject insulin
Type 2 diabetes: cells resistant to insulin → linked to obesity/poor diet → diet, exercise, medication
Glycogenesis = glucose → glycogen (storage); Glycogenolysis = glycogen → glucose (release)
Negative feedback: change → response that reverses the change → return to set point
Pancreas acts as both sensor and hormone-secreting organ in blood glucose control
